This stage is the main-loop action layer that turns model-requested tool calls into real, policy-controlled side effects. It sits between tool selection and the rest of the runtime: once the model asks to run something, this stage resolves the tool definition, decides whether it is exposed and callable, checks sandbox and approval requirements, executes the action through the appropriate backend, and converts the outcome back into protocol items the conversation can continue with.

Approval, guardian, and hook mediation is the gatekeeper: it evaluates permission requests, runs hooks, opens review flows when needed, and enforces allow/deny decisions in both UI and runtime. Execution backends and sandboxed command runtimes then carry out approved shell commands, patches, code-mode actions, and other process-like work under Unix or Windows confinement. Extension and integration tools supply externally backed capabilities such as MCP, plugins, connectors, web, image, memory, and skills, while sandbox-policy and command-safety helpers derive concrete confinement settings and classify commands before launch.

The directly assigned tools crate provides the shared contract for executable tools, schema normalization, MCP metadata conversion, error classification, and public APIs, while core tool-handler utilities supply common argument, path, environment, and permission-parsing helpers used across implementations.
